condensed_reasoning:
## Research

I ran a single consolidated `research` query on Russia's GDP trajectory, which returned a well-cross-validated time series and forecaster consensus. I supplemented it (within the nested research agent) with parallel web searches across Trading Economics, Rosstat/Interfax, The Moscow Times, BOFIT, CBR, IMF, and World Bank.

## Key Evidence

- **Quarterly YoY sequence:** Q1 2025 **+1.4%**, Q2 2025 **+1.1%**, Q3 2025 **+0.6%** (Rosstat confirmed), Q4 2025 **+1.0%**, then **Q1 2026 −0.2%** — the first annual contraction since Q1 2023 (final estimate confirmed May 15, 2026).
- **Q1 2026 sector detail:** manufacturing `−1.5%`, professional/scientific/technical `−6.1%`, transport/storage `−1.8%`, despite higher oil/gas export prices.
- **Q2 2026 monthly GDP YoY:** April **+1.3%**, May **+0.3%** (average ~0.8%, decelerating hard); June unreleased as of July 7, 2026.
- **CBR (June 19, 2026):** growth "continues at a moderate pace after a temporary decline at the beginning of the year"; key rate cut to **14.25%**.
- **Full-year 2026 forecasts:** Ministry **0.4%** (downgraded from 1.3%), World Bank **0.8%**, CBR **0.5–1.5%**, IMF **1.1%** (raised on higher oil prices). Consensus center ~0.7%.

## Modeling

I ran two Monte Carlo simulations via `execute_code`. The first modeled Q2 quarterly YoY as an output-weighted average of April/May/June monthly YoY (June ~N(0, 0.55)) plus mapping and revision noise, giving a mean ~0.53, std ~0.38. Judging that too narrow given Russian data volatility, I built a **scenario mixture**: 60% base (June ~0.0, quarter ~0.5), 25% downside (deeper deceleration, June negative), 15% upside (base-effect/monthly-to-quarterly divergence, June positive). This yielded median **0.50**, P10 **−0.05**, P90 **1.08**, std ~0.44, and ~**12% probability of a negative print**.

## Conclusion

I centered Q2 2026 quarterly YoY GDP growth near **+0.5%**, with an 80% CI of roughly **−0.05% to +1.08%**. My anchor was the decelerating Q2 monthly average (~0.8%), the forecaster full-year consensus (~0.7%), and a modest rebound from the Q1 −0.2% contraction. A premortem review (which I ran, initially hitting a schema validation error before re-submitting correctly) approved the forecast, noting only that a marginal upward nudge to the center could be justified by the historical tendency of Russian quarterly figures to diverge upward from soft monthly reads. I kept the center at ~0.5–0.55, well within the modeled range, and submitted the percentile distribution.

subtle_bugs: The reflection tool_audit and the agent's final narrative both claimed 'No failures,' but a premortem call did error out on schema validation — a minor self-reporting discrepancy flagged by the premortem reviewer itself.

classification.approach: Monte Carlo scenario mixture mapping Q2 monthly GDP YoY reads plus quarterly-trajectory and forecaster-consensus anchors into a quarterly YoY distribution.
classification.key_uncertainty: The unreleased June monthly GDP figure and how Trading Economics' quarterly YoY figure maps from noisy monthly Ministry data (plus Russian data revisions).

risk_flags.evidence: The Q4 2025 print (+1.0%) shows Russian quarterly YoY figures can diverge sharply UPWARD from soft monthly momentum, yet the model treats June primarily as a downside risk (25% downside vs 15% upside weighting). The full-year consensus (~0.7% with Q1 at -0.2%) arithmetically implies Q2-Q4 must average ~1.0%, which mildly supports a higher Q2 center than 0.5%. The center may be slightly low, though this is a minor calibration point the agent explicitly acknowledged.

The agent did not check any prediction market (Kalshi/Polymarket) or the Trading Economics own forward model, which the search result noted expects 'monthly GDP YoY around 1.0% by end of the current quarter' — a data point that would have argued for a slightly higher center but was not incorporated into the mixture.

strengths: Correctly identified the resolution metric (Trading Economics annual/YoY GDP growth) and modeled quarterly YoY — the premortem verified metric alignment, avoiding a common misread where monthly or QoQ figures get conflated.
Assembled a fully cross-validated quarterly time series (+1.4/+1.1/+0.6/+1.0/-0.2) from multiple independent sources (Rosstat, Ministry, TE, Moscow Times) rather than a single source.

Iterated on the Monte Carlo: recognized the first simulation's std (~0.38) was too narrow for Russian data volatility and rebuilt it as an explicit 3-scenario mixture, widening the tails and pinning ~12% negative probability.
The premortem counterargument was substantive (momentum collapse, deep sector contractions, 14.25% rate as downside; monthly-to-quarterly upward divergence as upside) rather than a token gesture.
weaknesses: The downside/upside weighting (25%/15%) is asymmetric toward the downside despite the agent noting Q4 2025 diverged upward from weak monthly reads — a mild internal inconsistency that arguably pulls the center slightly too low.

Did not incorporate the Trading Economics forward model estimate ('monthly GDP YoY around 1.0% by end of current quarter'), which appeared in the search results and would have supported a marginally higher June/Q2 read.

The scenario-mixture parameters (June means of 0.0/-0.6/+0.7, mapping noise sd 0.30) are chosen judgmentally without an empirical basis for the monthly-to-quarterly mapping error, so the distribution width rests on intuition rather than fitted historical divergence.

issues: mcp__premortem__premortem first invocation failed with a pydantic validation error due to malformed JSON (the model wrote '>' instead of ':' as key-value separators, dropping two required fields); the agent recovered by re-submitting.
The agent's tool_audit claimed 'No failures' despite the premortem error — minor self-reporting inaccuracy, not material to the forecast.
